1:
On Matthew 10:1 the witnesses read the calling and empowering of the Twelve as the answer to the prayer of the previous chapter (Matthew 9:38) — the Lord of the harvest now thrusting forth his first labourers.

The Latin fathers, through the Catena, dwell on the mystery of the number. Remigius calls twelve “a perfect number,” six (perfect in its own parts) doubled; Bede reads it as three into four, “that through the four quarters of the world they were to preach the faith of the holy Trinity”; Rabanus heaps up its Old Testament types — the twelve sons of Jacob, the twelve springs at Elim, the twelve stones of Aaron’s breastplate and the shewbread, and in the New the twelve stars of the bride’s crown and the foundations of the new Jerusalem. Augustine and Ambrose note that one among them was evil, chosen that the Church might learn to bear with wicked men. The eastern Chrysostom marks that he made them confident “not only by calling their ministry a sending forth to the harvest, but by giving them strength.”

The grammatical-historical commentators fix the facts: this is the mission, not the choice, of the Twelve (already made, Luke 6:13); the number “at once suggested… the twelve tribes” (Ellicott), and the exousia given is “authority” as much as power (Robertson). The Reformed Calvin presses that the appointment to preach and to work miracles is given “in immediate connection,” so that “miracles are nothing else than seals of his doctrine” — whence “the Papists… are guilty… by separating his word from miracles.” The Puritan Henry adds that Christ had kept them first in probation and then in preparation, the best preparative being “acquaintance and communion with Jesus Christ” (John 12:26). Jerome notes the wide difference “between having and imparting”: the apostles act only “with confession of their own weakness” (Acts 3:6). The power, all agree, is levelled against the devil and his kingdom — to conquer Satan and to cure the world.

Across the witnesses the verse marks the turn from the disciples’ long apprenticeship to their commission. Calvin notes this is not the first choosing — when Christ “selected them for admission into his private circle” — but the hour when “they must put their hands to the work,” endued “with the power of the Holy Spirit” lest there be “any want of authority.” The healing power, all agree, is no end in itself: it is “directly levelled against the devil and his kingdom” (Henry), a pledge that the gospel comes “to conquer the devil and to cure the world.” Bede’s reading of twelve as “three into four” gathers the whole intent — the faith of the Trinity carried “through the four quarters of the world” — so that the number itself preaches the universal mission soon to open.
2:
On Matthew 10:2 — the heading of the catalogue of names — the witnesses explain the title “apostle” and the significance of the order.

The grammatical-historical commentators define the word. Gill notes this is the first time the disciples are called “apostles” — “learners before; now… sent forth to preach publicly” — comparing the “messengers of the sanhedrim” sent to announce the new moon. Clarke traces apostolos to one “commissioned by a king to negotiate,” equal to a herald; and observes that “those who were Christ’s apostles were first his disciples… men must be first taught of God, before they be sent of God.” Ellicott’s long comparison of the four lists (Mark 3:16; Luke 6:13; Acts 1:13) draws out that Peter is always first and Judas always last, that the names fall into three fixed groups of four, and that Peter, James, and John formed the innermost circle, “chosen out of the chosen.” Robertson notes Matthew names them in pairs, “probably as they were sent out” two by two (Mark 6:7).

On the primacy of Peter the schools speak plainly. The grammatical-historical voices grant him “acknowledged preeminence” as first-called and spokesman, yet find “not the least mark of any supremacy” (Henry). The Reformed Calvin is sharpest: “The Church of Rome displays extreme folly in drawing from this passage their doctrine of the primacy”; that Simon was first “does not imply that he possessed authority over his companions,” and in any case his rank avails the Roman See nothing “till they prove that wicked and sacrilegious apostles are Peter’s successors.” The wesleyan-arminian Wesley adds the modest gloss that Simon was simply “the first who was called to a constant attendance on Christ.”

The witnesses dwell, too, on the grace of being named at all. Henry observes that of some — Bartholomew, Simon the Canaanite — “we know no more… than their names,” yet they were “faithful servants,” for “all the good ministers of Christ are not alike famous.” They are coupled, he notes, because “at first they were sent forth two and two… two are better than one,” and “out of the mouth of two witnesses every word would be established.” Three of the pairs were brethren — Peter and Andrew, James and John, the other James and Lebbaeus — so that “friendship and fellowship” among relations are “made serviceable to religion.” The list itself, the witnesses agree, was set down “for the truth of the history… and for the exclusion and detection of false apostles” (Gill) — a roll of honour whose deepest comfort is that these names are “written in heaven.”
3:
On Matthew 10:3 — Philip, Bartholomew, Thomas, Matthew, James, Lebbaeus — the witnesses are chiefly occupied with identifying the men and their double names.

The grammatical-historical commentators do the patient work of identification. Gill and Lightfoot, following the conjecture of the latter, hold that Bartholomew — “Bar-Talmai, the son of Talmai (or Ptolemy)” — is the same as Nathanael, since he falls in the lists at just the place Nathanael holds in John 1, named by his own name there and by his father’s here. Barnes notes that “Lebbaeus” and “Thaddaeus” “have the same signification in Hebrew,” while Luke calls the same man Judas — “such changes are common.” Lightfoot adds the geographical guesses, deriving Lebbaeus from the coastal town of Lebba in Galilee, and notes that Alphaeus and Cleophas are one name, so that “Mary the wife of Cleophas” (John 19:25) is the mother of James the Less.

On Matthew himself the witnesses note the evangelist’s humility. Clarke marks that here, “writing of himself,” Matthew styles himself “the publican” — the badge of his former life — and (as Henry observes) places Thomas before himself, where Mark and Luke had put Matthew first; “it well becomes the disciples of Christ in honour to prefer one another.” The wesleyan-arminian Wesley keeps the note brief, identifying Lebbaeus as “commonly called Judas, the brother of James.” Across the voices the lesson drawn from this bare list is that obscure names — men of whom Scripture records little more — are nonetheless written in heaven, while “the high and mighty names of the great ones of the earth are buried in the dust” (Luke 10:20).

The convergence of the witnesses on these obscurer names yields a quiet lesson. Henry marks that Matthew, ordained before Thomas in the lists of Mark and Luke, here “in his own catalogue” sets Thomas first and brands himself “the publican” — that the called of Christ may “look unto the rock whence they were hewn… and divine grace may be the more glorified.” The patient identifications — Bartholomew with Nathanael, Lebbaeus with Thaddaeus and Judas, James with the son of Alphaeus and Cleophas — are not idle, for they knit the Gospel testimony together and exclude the “false Apostles” Jerome warned of. The abiding note the witnesses sound is that men of whom Scripture says little were yet, by their office, made the “twelve foundations” of the new Jerusalem (Revelation 21:14), their honour not of earth but of heaven.
4:
On Matthew 10:4 — “Simon the Canaanite, and Judas Iscariot, who also betrayed him” — the witnesses explain the two surnames that close the list.

The grammatical-historical commentators are unanimous that “the Canaanite” is no ethnic label. Gill, Lightfoot, Barnes, and Clarke alike derive it from the Hebrew kana, “zealous,” which Luke renders literally as “Zelotes” (Luke 6:15). Gill describes the Zealots as a party who, “in imitation of Phinehas,” would summarily strike down open offenders out of a pretended zeal for God’s honour — so that to say Simon was once of this sect does him “no more wrong” than to call his brother Matthew a publican. The name distinguishes him, all note, from Simon Peter.

On Judas the witnesses agree the surname is most likely geographical. Barnes, Clarke, and Robertson take “Iscariot” as Ish-Kerioth, “a man of Kerioth,” a town of Judah (Joshua 15:25) — which, as Ellicott observed, would make him the one apostle of Judah among Galileans. Lightfoot and Clarke note the alternative conjecture, that the name was given after his death, from a word for strangulation, since he hanged himself. The wesleyan-arminian Clarke also presses a point of translation: “who also betrayed him” is better “even he who betrayed him,” lest it seem others betrayed him too. Jerome, in the Catena tradition, had heard in “Iscariot” a prophetic omen — the tribe of Issachar meaning “a booty,” figuring “the reward of the betrayer.” That the traitor is named without disguise, the witnesses note, shows Christ knew from the first whom he had chosen.

The witnesses find in this closing pair a double lesson of grace. That Simon the Zealot — once, it seems, of a party that struck down offenders “in imitation of Phinehas” — could be made an apostle shows, as Gill notes, that the calling “does him no more wrong” than naming Matthew a publican: grace reaches the most zealous and the most despised alike. And that Judas Iscariot stands in the company at all, named last and branded “who also betrayed him,” the witnesses take, with Henry, as proof that “from the first, Christ knew what a wretch he was… yet… took him among the apostles,” that the Church might “not be a surprise and discouragement” when “the vilest scandals should break out in the best societies.” Tares grow among the wheat, wolves among the sheep, until “a day of discovery and separation” comes.
5:
On Matthew 10:5 — “Go not into the way of the Gentiles… but rather to the lost sheep of the house of Israel” — the witnesses are agreed on the principle of the Jew-first mission and divide only on its rationale.

The eastern and Latin fathers press the pastoral and apologetic motives. Chrysostom marks the propriety of the timing — the apostles sent only after they had seen the dead raised and the sea rebuked — and reads the restriction as a kindness: trained first “in Judaea, as in a palaestra,” like “weak nestlings” taught to fly, and sent as physicians to the very people who had reviled Christ. Jerome adds that the coming of Christ “behoved… to be preached to the Jews first, that they might not… say that they were rejected”; the Samaritans, “Gentiles… settled in the land” by the king of Assyria, stood at enmity with the Jews. Gregory and Hilary add that Israel, having had “more care in being warned” through the Law, would have “less excuse for its crime.”

The grammatical-historical and Reformed commentators ground the order in covenant. Henry notes “the Gentiles must not have the gospel… till the Jews have first refused it” (Acts 3:26), the restraint binding “only in their first mission.” Calvin makes covenant the whole reason: Christ was “the minister of circumcision, to fulfill the promises… given to the fathers” (Romans 15:8); God “established with the seed of Abraham alone his covenant,” and only after the resurrection spread the blessing to all nations. Wesley simply notes the calling of the Gentiles was “deferred till after… pentecost.” The Gloss adds a sharp aside — the command to preach freely was given “that Judas who had the bag might not use the… power for getting money.”

So the restriction, far from unkindness, is read by all the witnesses as the order of covenant mercy. Henry gathers the principle: “the first offer of salvation must be made to the Jews” (Acts 3:26), and only when they refuse it does the gospel pass to the Gentiles — “if the gospel be hid from any place, Christ thereby hides himself from that place.” The fathers add the apologetic edge: by going first to Israel, every plea of rejection was stopped, and the way to the Gentiles was opened the more justly when the chosen people spurned their hour. Wesley notes the prohibition touched only preaching, not common dealings, for the disciples “might” enter a Samaritan town “to buy what they wanted” (John 4:9). The restraint, all agree, was for a season only — the same lips later sent to “teach all nations.”
6:
On Matthew 10:6 — “but go rather to the lost sheep of the house of Israel” — the witnesses dwell on the tenderness of the word “lost” and the breadth of “the house of Israel.”

The grammatical-historical commentators define the terms. Gill explains “the house of Israel” as “the whole Jewish nation” then in the land, not the ten tribes only; and “the lost sheep” as either the people at large, “wandering, and… lost in error and sin,” or particularly “the elect of God among them.” Barnes paints them “wandering and lost, like sheep straying without a shepherd” (Ezekiel 34:5; Isaiah 53:6), to whom “it was proper that the gospel should be first offered.” Robertson notes a fine point of feeling: Jesus uses “lost” “not in blame, but in pity,” and (with Bengel) that he says “lost” more often than “led astray.” Clarke adds that “the sheep had wandered from their Shepherd,” and Christ sends “these under-shepherds to seek, find, and bring them back.”

The Reformed Calvin draws out both the covenant priority and a universal warning. The first rank is given the Jews “because they were the firstborn,” the only people then “acknowledged by God to belong to his family”; the word “lost” is meant partly to move the apostles to “warmer affection” toward them. But “under the figure of this nation, Christ taught what is the condition of the whole human race”: if the Jews, “near to God, and in covenant with him… the lawful heirs of eternal life,” are nonetheless “lost” till they “regain salvation through Christ,” how much more those who “have not such a prerogative.” The shepherdless straying of Israel, all the voices agree, is the mirror of every soul outside of Christ.

The witnesses make this single clause a window on the whole gospel. Christ, says Henry, “had a particular and very tender concern for the house of Israel,” beloved “for the fathers’ sakes” (Romans 11:28), and looked on them “with compassion… as lost sheep” whom, as a shepherd, he would gather “out of the by-paths of sin and error.” The description was given, he adds, “to quicken them to diligence,” for the apostles were sent to the very people “of which number they themselves lately were.” Calvin draws the wider warning home: the Gentiles too “had been as lost sheep” (1 Peter 2:25), and the whole race is mirrored in this shepherdless nation — none, however near to God by privilege, is safe until “gathered into the fold” by Christ the one Shepherd.
7:
On Matthew 10:7 — “as ye go, preach, saying, The kingdom of heaven is at hand” — the witnesses note the itinerant manner and the identity of the message.

The grammatical-historical commentators stress that this is the same proclamation already heard from the Baptist and from Christ (Matthew 3:2; Matthew 4:17). Robertson renders the participle and imperative as the work of “itinerant preachers on a preaching tour, heralds… proclaiming good news”; they were “to shake Galilee with it as Jesus had done.” Ellicott suspects “a formula of proclamation”: the envoys were to enter a town, “standing in the gate, to announce that His kingdom had come near,” and then to call the gathered crowds to repentance. Clarke, citing Quesnel, unfolds the phrase — “the kingdom, to animate the hope; of heaven, to inspire the love of heavenly things… which is at hand, that men may prepare for it without delay.”

The Reformed Calvin reads the announcement as designed “to arouse the minds of the nation to expect an approaching redemption” — to inform the Jews that their restoration was “divine agency, and not… the kindness of men,” and that the promised happiness was “not earthly and fading, but heavenly and eternal.” The eastern Chrysostom (whose note here is partly displaced onto the previous chapter’s matter of John’s disciples) emphasises that Christ “knew the secrets of all men” and set right their understanding. The Puritan and Reformed voices agree that the apostles need not say only these words but must make this their text — the morning light announcing the rising sun, that the kingdom long looked for was now to be set up.

The witnesses note how the proclamation binds the apostles’ message to the whole stream of revelation. It is “the same that John the Baptist and Christ had preached before” (Henry), so that men “need to have good truths pressed again and again upon them.” The kingdom announced is “not so much the personal presence of the king… but a spiritual kingdom… set up… in the hearts of men” — and from its nearness flows the call “that men should repent” (Mark 6:12). Unlike Jonah’s cry of ruin, this “proclaims salvation at hand”: “mercy and truth meet together” (Psalm 85:10). The kingdom that was then “at hand” is now come, the witnesses observe, yet still must be preached — men told that “it is come to them,” and quickened toward the kingdom of glory yet to come.
8:
On Matthew 10:8 — “Heal the sick… freely ye have received, freely give” — the witnesses treat both the works of mercy and the charge against avarice, and note a textual question.

The grammatical-historical commentators flag the words “raise the dead.” Clarke and Ellicott report that the best manuscripts omit them, “more in accordance with the facts of the Gospel history, which records no instance” of the disciples raising the dead before the resurrection; the insertion may reflect later cases like Dorcas (Acts 9:40) and Eutychus (Acts 20:9). On “freely give” they agree the Greek is stronger than the English — “give as a free gift, give gratis” (Ellicott) — though this never forbids a competent support (1 Corinthians 9:14; 1 Timothy 5:18).

The eastern and Latin fathers read the gift theologically. Chrysostom hears in “freely ye have received” a check on pride and a command to keep “pure from filthy lucre”: they bestow nothing of their own, having received without money. Remigius allegorizes the works — “the sick” are the slothful, “the lepers” the unclean in sin, “the daemoniacs” those under the devil’s power. The Reformed Calvin sees in the healing miracles “an analogy… to the office of Christ,” who came “to bestow upon us every blessing”; the apostles are “channels for transmitting the bounty of God,” and “all hirelings basely corrupt… the sacred office” (Isaiah 55:1). Clarke calls preaching for hire “the most infamous sacrilege,” and the misattached Cyril fragment on Gehazi’s sold cure (2 Kings 5:26) makes the same point from the opposite side.

The witnesses gather the verse into a portrait of the gospel’s freeness. Henry notes the apostles were “sent abroad as public blessings,” their useful miracles showing “that love and goodness were the spirit and genius of that gospel” — works not of “destruction” like some of Moses’s, nor of “ostentation,” but “all for edification.” The charge “freely give” makes the kingdom one “not only of grace, but of free grace” (Romans 3:24): those who could have enriched themselves by such cures must “cure gratis,” that none mistake the gospel for merchandise. Simon Magus, the witnesses recall, “would not have offered money… if he had not hoped to get money” by the gifts of the Spirit (Acts 8:18) — the very sin this word forbids. “Freely you have received” remains the spring of all free giving.
9:
On Matthew 10:9 — “Provide neither gold, nor silver, nor brass in your purses” — the witnesses debate how far the rule binds, and read the freedom from baggage as freedom from care.

The eastern Chrysostom makes the prohibition a deliverance: having forbidden them to make merchandise of holy things, Christ “proceeds to pull up the root of all evil,” freeing the apostles “from all suspicions… from all care,” so they may give their whole time to preaching — “a happy exchange” of gold for the power to heal and raise. The Latin fathers read the same austerity, Jerome glancing at the begging philosophers (the Bactroperatae) who “carry a bag about with them,” and offering also a figurative sense — gold for sense, silver for words, brass for voice — not to be received from heretics but given by the Lord. Hilary spiritualizes the girdle, the scrip, the two coats, the shoes, and the staff as detachments from worldly care, heresy, and “extraneous power, having a rod from the root of Jesse” (Isaiah 11:1). Augustine carefully holds that the precept “does not at all imply” that ministers may live only by such contributions — else “Paul transgressed” by his own hands — for Christ “bestows a privilege” one may decline.

The grammatical-historical and Reformed voices restrict the command to this mission. The tense of “provide” (ktēsēsthe, “do not acquire,” Robertson, Ellicott) means “if they had money, they might take it,” but not get more or delay to get it; “in your purses” is literally “in your girdles,” the hollow belt used as a purse. Calvin insists the injunction “must undoubtedly be restricted” to the temporary commission, lest men “ignorantly” make it “a perpetual” rule. Clarke turns it inward: anxiety in Christ’s messenger is “a double crime,” insinuating “a bad opinion of the Master” who is bound to support those he sends.

The witnesses see in the stripping of provision a deliberate schooling. Henry reads it both as “the conduct of human prudence” — they were on “a short excursion” and need not be burdened — and as a lesson “to act in dependence upon Divine Providence,” living “without taking thought for life” (Matthew 6:25). Those who go “upon Christ’s errand have, of all people, most reason to trust him for food convenient,” being “taken under special protection” and “entitled to special provisions.” The deeper point, pressed by Chrysostom, is that to be freed “from all anxiety of this life” is no hardship but a happiness — making the apostles “Angels more than men,” with “but one care, that of teaching.” The empty purse, far from leaving them destitute, was the badge of men whose Master had pledged to supply their every want.
10:
On Matthew 10:10 — “nor scrip… nor two coats, neither shoes, nor yet staves: for the workman is worthy of his meat” — the witnesses harmonize the apparent contradiction with Mark and ground the maxim of maintenance.

The grammatical-historical commentators do the harmonizing. Where Matthew and Luke forbid a staff and Mark allows one, Barnes and Clarke (the latter preferring the reading “staves,” plural) explain that the spirit is one: the apostles were to “go just as they were, to trust to Providence,” not to procure a second staff or second shoes “in case the first should break” (Ellicott). The “scrip,” Gill, Barnes, and Ellicott note, is the traveller’s wallet or food-basket — such as David’s (1 Samuel 17:40) — the “two coats” a change of raiment, the “shoes” here distinct from the peasant’s sandals which Mark permits. Robertson adds that “the sermon is worth the dinner.”

On “the workman is worthy of his meat” the schools converge while applying it variously. Chrysostom hears it as Christ’s tender provision — calling them “labourers” and their support their “hire,” that they “might not be ashamed” to receive necessaries. Augustine reads it as a soldier’s stipend, “these things were due to them from those to whom they preached.” The grammatical-historical Ellicott marks the “singular instance” that the same saying which here grounds Christ’s command to take no thought is quoted by Paul (1 Timothy 5:18) to ground “an organised system” of stipend — “the same law fulfils itself in many ways.” Clarke and Calvin agree the minister has “a Divine right” to maintenance — but only maintenance, “not to make a fortune.”

The witnesses make the harmonized detail serve a single end — the haste and trust of men wholly given to their errand. They were to go “like men in haste, and eager to begin… the ministry” (Clarke), procuring nothing that would “retard them” (Wesley). Yet the maxim “the workman is worthy of his meat” secures their bread: ministers “must be workmen, labourers,” and such “are worthy of their meat” (Henry), entitled, like those who “serve at the altar,” to live — though not to “grow rich” — by their work (1 Corinthians 9:14). The witnesses note the providential balance: they were “not to distrust their God” nor “distrust their countrymen,” trusting God to “incline the hearts of those they went among” to supply what the empty purse and bare provision had left them lacking.
11:
On Matthew 10:11 — “enquire who in it is worthy; and there abide till ye go thence” — the witnesses read both a practical rule of lodging and a caution against fickleness.

The eastern Chrysostom asks how this squares with Christ’s own lodging with a publican, and answers that “worthy” here “had respect not to their rank, but to their furnishing food”: the host need only provide bare necessaries. He notes that Christ has not yet given them power “to discern who is worthy,” so they must “seek out” by report; and the command to remain in one house keeps them from grieving a host or incurring “suspicion of lightness or gluttony.” The Latin fathers agree: Jerome bids them choose “by the report of the people… that the worthiness of the preacher might not be disgraced by the ill character of his entertainer,” the host being one who “rather receives than does a favour.” Ambrose adds that the caution binds the guest, not the host, lest “hospitality should be diminished.”

The grammatical-historical and Reformed commentators turn the rule to diligence. Barnes reads “there abide” against wandering “in the manner of vagrants and mendicants” — ministers are useful not by “idle chitchat” but by “an honest and laborious improvement of every moment in study, prayer, preaching.” Clarke presses the same: “Go not about from house to house” (Luke 10:7); “seldom frequent the tables of the rich,” for “the unction of God will perish” and the preaching become “a dry barren repetition.” Calvin grounds the rule in haste: they were to “begin with those who… were better prepared,” and not to change lodging lest they burden any one, “so that… they may immediately run to another” city.

The witnesses read in the rule a charge to be both discerning and settled. There would be, even “in the worst of times and places,” some “better than their neighbours… as wheat among the chaff” — “there were saints in Nero’s household” (Henry); the apostles were to “ask for the good people of the place” as Paul “found out the brethren” (Acts 28:14). Having found such, they were to “continue” there, not changing quarters, for “they are justly suspected… that are often changing.” The deeper aim, all agree, was that the messengers should appear neither as wandering beggars nor as men of idleness, but as those with “regular, important business,” valuing their time and giving themselves “to labour, prayer, and meditation” — so that the manner of their lodging itself commended the gospel they bore.
12:
On Matthew 10:12 — “and when ye come into an house, salute it” — the witnesses explain both the form of the greeting and its courtesy.

The grammatical-historical commentators correct the English. Barnes and Ellicott note “an house” should be “the house,” the dwelling already found worthy, and that “house” means “family” (compare John 4:53). The salutation, implied by what follows, is the familiar “Peace be to this house” (Luke 10:5). Barnes draws the social lesson: “Religion never requires or permits its friends to outrage the common rules of social intercourse,” but demands “the customary and proper tokens of respect” (1 Peter 2:17; Philippians 4:8). Clarke expands the Hebrew shalom as comprehending “all blessings, spiritual and temporal” — “great is peace, for all other blessings are comprehended in it” — so that to wish peace “in the name… of Christ” was “a positive promise… of all the good implied,” and he illustrates from the Eastern traveller who simply announces himself a guest for the night.

The Reformed Calvin reads the greeting as a trial. Unable to “distinguish the devout… from despisers,” the apostles are to “address in a friendly manner any family” they meet, the salutation being “a kind of opening to a conversation”: “make trial, when you first enter, whether your entertainers will cheerfully submit to hear you.” The Gloss, in the Catena, glosses it briefly as a prayer “that all resistance to the truth may be pacified.” Wesley keeps to the form — peace, “that is, all blessings, be to this house.” The courteous greeting, the voices agree, is both a real benediction and a means of discerning where the message will be welcome.

The witnesses make the simple greeting a school of evangelical courtesy. Though the apostles went “backed with the authority of the Son of God himself,” their instruction was, on entering a house, “not to command it, but to salute it” (Henry), for “love’s sake rather to beseech, is the evangelical way” (Philemon 9). Souls are “first drawn to Christ with the cords of a man, and kept to him by the bands of love” (Hosea 11:4). The salutation served also “to draw on further discourse,” passing from “matters of common conversation” into “that communication which is good to the use of edifying,” and to “try whether you are welcome,” for “he that will not receive your salutation kindly, will not receive your message kindly” (Luke 16:10). Religion, all agree, “teaches us to be courteous and civil” to all.
13:
On Matthew 10:13 — “if the house be worthy, let your peace come upon it: but if it be not worthy, let your peace return to you” — the witnesses read the benediction as something real that is given or withdrawn.

The grammatical-historical commentators explain the idiom. Barnes calls the returning peace “a Hebrew mode” (compare Psalm 35:13), “derived from bestowing a gift”: if the family receive it, they have its benefit; if not, “the present came back… in the hand of the giver.” If rejected and persecuted, “the blessings which they sought for others would come upon themselves” (Matthew 5:10). Clarke renders it that the peace “shall turn back upon yourselves — they shall get nothing, and you shall have an increase.” Ellicott meets the difficulty of the “if” — how can worthiness still be doubtful after enquiry? — by noting that the missionaries entered “as strangers,” so “even the most careful inquiry might not always be successful”; the imperative is “the proclamation of an edict from the King,” not a mere form, and “at the worst, the prayer for peace would bring a blessing on him who prayed.” Robertson asks what makes a house worthy and answers, “readiness to receive the preachers and their message.”

The Reformed Calvin states the principle: ingratitude “makes them unworthy to enjoy the blessing of God which you have supplicated for them,” so the apostles are to “break off every bond of communication.” He cautions, however, against too fine a refinement — it is “too great… speculation” to make the verse refer to “the free reconciliation… between God and men” rather than to the customary Jewish wish of well-being. The peace offered, all the witnesses agree, is no empty courtesy: it is a real gift that finds its rest in the worthy and returns, undiminished, to bless the messenger when it is refused.

The witnesses dwell on the reality of the peace thus offered. The common Eastern greeting, “as they used it, was turned into gospel” — “the peace of God, the peace of the kingdom of heaven” (Henry). Lest the apostles scruple to pronounce so great a blessing “upon all promiscuously,” many being unworthy, Christ frees them of the scruple: the prayer “should be put up for all,” and would find its rest only where it was due. The witnesses note the comfort in the returning peace — the messenger’s labour is never lost, for if the house refuse the blessing, “it shall turn back upon yourselves” (Clarke), and the rejected sufferer reaps the benefit of being cast out “for my Master’s sake” (Matthew 5:10). What seems a mere courtesy is thus, on every reading, a real and weighty gift.
14:
On Matthew 10:14 — “whosoever shall not receive you… shake off the dust of your feet” — the witnesses are unanimous on the meaning of the symbolic act and grave on the warning it carries.

The grammatical-historical commentators explain the gesture from Jewish custom. Lightfoot, Barnes, Clarke, and Ellicott alike note that the rabbis held even “the dust of a heathen land” to defile; to shake it off, therefore, was “a significant act,” declaring the place “impure, profane, and heathenish… unworthy of their instruction” (Barnes), and protesting that a city which would not receive the messengers of Christ was “below the level even of the Gentiles” (Ellicott). They record that the apostles literally did this (Acts 13:51; Acts 18:6). Robertson calls it “a rather violent gesture of disfavour,” and notes again that the rule, like the rest, belonged to “this special tour with its peculiar perils.”

The Latin fathers and Reformed draw out the severity. Hilary reads in the shaken dust “an eternal curse… left” on those who reject the kingdom, all fellowship with the place renounced; Rabanus, by contrast, takes the dust as “the lightness of earthly thoughts” the preachers gather, turned now “as a witness” against the despisers’ damnation. Calvin makes the verse a measure of the gospel’s worth: it “shows in what estimation the Lord holds his gospel,” an “inestimable treasure” and “the scepter of his kingdom,” so that “no crime is more offensive to God than contempt of his word” — whence Christ enjoins so solemn a mode of execration as he does for no adulterer or murderer. The threat, Calvin adds, was also a comfort, that the apostles “might not fail in the middle of their course” (2 Corinthians 2:16).

The witnesses press the gravity of the warning. The shaken dust is “a testimony… that in preaching the Gospel they had come even so far” (Jerome), and that “from those that rejected the Gospel they would accept nothing, not even the necessaries of life.” Jerome marks the dreadful comparison that follows — “to the men of Sodom and Gomorrah no man had ever preached; but this city had been preached to and had rejected the Gospel” — whence “there is difference of degree in the punishment of sinners.” Rabanus turns the gesture inward: the dust is “the lightness of earthly thoughts” even the greatest doctors gather as they tread “the ways of this world,” now witnessing against those who despised their word. The act, on every reading, is no petulance but a solemn protest, leaving the rejecter without excuse and the gospel’s honour vindicated.
15:
On Matthew 10:15 — “it shall be more tolerable for the land of Sodom and Gomorrha in the day of judgment, than for that city” — the witnesses agree on the dreadful comparison and divide only on the day intended.

The grammatical-historical commentators set the measure by light. Barnes notes that Sodom and Gomorrha, destroyed for their wickedness (Genesis 19:24), will yet find their punishment “more easily borne” than that of gospel-rejecters, “because they were not favoured with so much light” (compare Matthew 11:24). Robertson glosses “more tolerable” from the vernacular — the Galileans “having more privileges than Sodom and Gomorrha had.” Ellicott observes that “the day of judgment,” like the Old Testament “day of the Lord,” is wider than our usual thought, including “the earlier and more earthly judgments, as well as… the great consummation.”

On precisely which day, the schools part. Clarke leans toward “a day in which God should send punishment on that particular city,” the “day of judgment of Sodom” being the time of its fiery overthrow. The Reformed Calvin rejects any reference to the destruction of Jerusalem as “foreign to our Lord’s intention,” insisting it means the final day in which “both must give their account, that there may be a comparison of the punishments.” Calvin draws the weight of the saying: to reject the gospel — to deny the authority of him “who made and formed” us and “reject disdainfully his gentle invitations” — is “the utmost accumulation… of all crimes,” so that if Sodom’s lesser sin met such vengeance, “how awful must be the punishment that awaits those who reject Christ when he speaks openly.” The misattached Cyril fragment, on the many proofs of life eternal by faith and by the preaching of the gospel, sits beside the verse as a stray.

The witnesses make the comparison a sober measure of privilege and guilt. The principle, drawn out by all, is that judgment is proportioned to light: the cities most “conspicuous for their infamy” were “yet less guilty… than those who rejected the messengers of the King” (Ellicott), the same comparison reappearing with Tyre and Sidon (Matthew 11:22). Sodom was chosen, Calvin notes, not only because it “went beyond… all in flagitious crimes” but because God destroyed it “in an extraordinary manner, that it might serve as an example to all ages.” If even “the rejection of that obscure preaching” of the apostles met such vengeance, “how awful must be the punishment” of those who reject Christ “when he speaks openly” — and worse still the lot of “furious enemies” who persecute the gospel “by fire and sword.”
16:
On Matthew 10:16 — “Behold, I send you forth as sheep in the midst of wolves: be ye therefore wise as serpents, and harmless as doves” — the witnesses dwell on the paradox of the charge and the consolation hidden in “I send.”

The eastern Chrysostom makes the emphatic “Lo, I send you” their chief comfort: not merely “to wolves” but “in the midst of wolves,” to show his power, that the sheep “should not only prevail over the wolves, but… convert them” — “a much greater… power that can change their hearts than that can kill them.” The dove’s nature is “not to retaliate,” for “anger is extinguished not by anger, but by meekness.” The Latin fathers agree the preacher must “not do evil, but suffer it” (Gregory); the serpent “hides his head… that he may protect the part in which life is,” so we guard “our head which is Christ” and keep the faith uncorrupt (Jerome); and wisdom and simplicity must be joined, since “simplicity without wisdom might be easily deceived, and wisdom is dangerous unless… tempered with simplicity” (Remigius).

The grammatical-historical commentators draw out the double figure. Barnes finds the point of likeness to the serpent in “its caution in avoiding danger,” and the dove “a striking emblem of innocence.” Ellicott and Robertson press the paradox: the disciples are to be “at once supremely guileful and absolutely guileless,” akeraioi meaning “unmixed, unalloyed” — as Paul “caught men with guile” (2 Corinthians 12:16) yet without baser motive. Ellicott adds that Christ had seen the Spirit descend “like a dove” (Matthew 3:16), in whom “the two qualities… are reconciled.” The Reformed Calvin reads the verse as opening the discourse beyond the first mission to “the whole course of their apostleship”: the wolves are “the enraged enemies of the gospel,” and the serpent’s caution is to be “so regulated” that they are neither “more timid than is necessary” nor “more sluggish in duty.”

The witnesses hold together what seems to pull apart — clear foresight of suffering with full assurance of victory. Chrysostom marvels that men “who had never been beyond the lake in which they fished” did not “straightway depart from Him on hearing these things”; it was, he says, “the wisdom of their Teacher,” who “to each evil… attaches somewhat of alleviation” — here the words “for my sake” and “for a testimony.” The double charge guards against the two ruinous extremes: simplicity without wisdom is “easily deceived,” and wisdom without simplicity is “dangerous” (Remigius); the dove’s prudence “not to retaliate” tempers the serpent’s craft to escape. Calvin warns that those who would pass for cautious are “for the most part, timorous and lazy”; the regulated caution Christ enjoins is meant to keep them moving “honestly wherever their calling leads,” neither rushing into needless danger nor renouncing the call to avoid it.
17:
On Matthew 10:17 — “but beware of men: for they will deliver you up to the councils, and they will scourge you in their synagogues” — the witnesses identify the courts and the punishment and weigh the force of “men.”

The grammatical-historical commentators ground the detail. Gill, Clarke, and Robertson take “men” as the wolves of the previous verse — especially the rulers, “the Scribes, Pharisees, elders, and chief priests” — against whom the disciples must be on guard. The “councils” are not the great Sanhedrin but, by the plural, “the lesser councils connected with provincial synagogues” (Ellicott), the local tribunals of every town (Robertson). On scourging, Lightfoot notes that “in every synagogue there was a civil triumvirate” with power to scourge; Barnes details the law of forty stripes save one (Deuteronomy 25:3) and the three-lashed scourge that made thirty-nine, with which “Paul was five times scourged” (2 Corinthians 11:24; Acts 22:19). Ellicott and others note the punishment was inflicted “within the walls of the building,” which seemed no desecration to the Jews.

The Reformed Calvin debates the article. Erasmus had read “these men” (the wolves just described), but Calvin prefers to take it “indefinite,” a declaration “that caution ought to be exercised in dealing with men, among whom every thing is full of snares.” This caution, he insists, is “not that terror… which would keep them from discharging their duty,” but a foresight that their minds “might be early prepared” — for the excessively cautious become “timorous and lazy,” while the heedlessly secure “give way at the critical moment.” The contests foreseen, he adds, reach far beyond the first journey, “not in Judea only, but in more distant places.”

The witnesses turn the warning into settled preparation. The foresight Christ gives is not meant “to abate the fervor” of zeal (Calvin) but to keep the disciples from being “surprised by unexpected afflictions” that “reduce many to a state of weakness.” He “sounds the trumpet to them, that they may quickly make ready for the battle,” so that the dread of standing before princes — where “not only fear, but even shame, sometimes overpowers well-regulated minds” — might not unman them. Henry adds the bitter aggravation that the danger comes from “men… bone of their bone” — homo homini lupus, man a wolf to man — and from “professing men” who scourge them in their very synagogues, “places of meeting for the worship of God.” To be forewarned, all agree, was to be forearmed, that the blow when it fell might confirm rather than shake their faith.
18:
On Matthew 10:18 — “and ye shall be brought before governors and kings for my sake, for a testimony against them and the Gentiles” — the witnesses read both a precise prophecy and the public purpose of the suffering.

The grammatical-historical commentators mark the words as “a striking proof of the prescience of Christ” (Clarke). Gill notes that “governors” means the Roman magistrates — Paul before Gallio, Felix, and Festus — since the power of life and death had been taken from the Jewish council; the fulfilment, says Barnes, runs through the Acts (Acts 24:1), Peter said to stand before Nero, John before Domitian, “few things… more improbable” than that “the fishermen of Galilee would stand before the… monarchs of the East and the West.” On the phrase “against them,” Ellicott corrects the English: it is “rather, unto them,” the dative of the person addressed, “not involving necessarily any hostile… purpose” — a testimony rendered “both to Jews and Gentiles” (Clarke).

The eastern-alexandrian Origen widens the reference, observing that this saying — like “brother shall deliver up brother” — “perhaps… not to the Apostles alone” applied, but “to all who were about to believe the word.” Ellicott too hears the verse “looking forward… to that future work among the Gentiles upon which the Twelve were told that they were not as yet to enter” (Matthew 10:5). The Puritan Henry gathers the consolation: their being “hurried… from one judgment-seat to another” would “make their testimony the more public,” for God’s ministers are his witnesses (Isaiah 43:10) “not only in their doing work, but in their suffering work” — whence they are called martyrs, witnesses both for Christ and against those who oppose him.

The witnesses gather the verse as both prophecy and consolation. Its precise fulfilment — fishermen of Galilee standing before proconsuls and emperors — is, all agree, “a signal evidence that Christ possessed a knowledge of the future” (Barnes). And the suffering itself is given a purpose: their being dragged from one judgment-seat to another would “make their testimony the more public” and open “the way of believing on Christ” to the Gentiles (Hilary, on the kindred verse), so that the confessors preached “among the flames of persecution.” Henry presses the comfort that God’s witnesses serve him “not only in their doing work, but in their suffering work” (Isaiah 43:10); their sufferings “witness to the truth of the gospel” and stand as “testimony of the enmity of their persecutors” — evidence to be “produced… in the great day.”
19:
On Matthew 10:19 — “take no thought how or what ye shall speak: for it shall be given you in that same hour” — the witnesses read a tender promise of inspiration to anxious and unlettered men.

The Latin and eastern fathers raise the apostles to the rank of the prophets. Chrysostom answers their unspoken fear — “How shall we be able to persuade such men?” — by bidding them “be of good courage”: where a dispute is among friends we are told to “be ready” to give a reason (1 Peter 3:15), but “before the awful judgment… aid is ministered by Christ.” Remigius parses “how or what” as expression and substance, both supplied by him: “you utter the words, but it is I who speak” (2 Corinthians 13:3). Jerome adds that “we ought to offer only our will,” and “the grace of the Holy Spirit will minister in our answer”; Hilary likens it to Abraham, who, having given up Isaac, did not lack a ram.

The grammatical-historical and Reformed commentators stress that this is no charge to neglect study but a promise for the hour of trial. “Take no thought” means “be not anxious” (Barnes, the same word as Matthew 6:25); Clarke warns the promise does not “dispense with the obligation… to prepare ourselves by… the study of the Holy Scriptures, and by prayer,” and Robertson insists “Christ is not talking about preparation of sermons,” the injunction applying “to cases when preparation… would be impossible.” Ellicott points to “the courage of Peter and John before the Sanhedrin” as “the earliest and the most striking instance” (Acts 4:13). Calvin adds the moving testimony of the martyrs of his own day, men “almost devoid of talent” who, when called to confess, “exhibited a command of… graceful language altogether miraculous” — yet not wholly without care, for it was good they should “supplicate… that the Spirit might be given.”

The witnesses are careful to bound the promise rightly. It does not, Clarke insists, “dispense with the obligation… to prepare ourselves by the meditation of sacred truths, by the study of the Holy Scriptures, and by prayer”; it banishes only “distrust and inquietude on dangerous occasions.” Robertson agrees Christ “is not talking about preparation of sermons,” the word applying to “cases when preparation… would be impossible” — the sudden hour before kings. The fathers raise the dignity of the promise to its height: the apostles are made like “the Prophets, who have spoken by the Spirit of God” (Chrysostom), so that anxiety would argue distrust. Calvin notes the providential pattern — God often leaves believers “destitute of the gift of eloquence” until the hour of testimony, when those “who formerly appeared to be dumb” are “endued… with more than ordinary eloquence.”
20:
On Matthew 10:20 — “for it is not ye that speak, but the Spirit of your Father which speaketh in you” — the witnesses ground the promise of the previous verse in the indwelling Spirit, with one caution against over-reading it.

The grammatical-historical commentators take the words as strong but bounded. Gill explains the nicely-balanced sense: not that they would not speak — “both the things they spoke, and the very words… were… suggested and dictated by the Spirit of God”; the Spirit “the efficient cause, they… only instruments,” for “the preparation of the heart… and the answer of the tongue… were both from the Lord.” Clarke calls it “an extraordinary promise… literally fulfilled to those first preachers,” essential because “the New Testament dispensation was to be fully opened by their… inspiration” — yet adds that “in a certain measure” the Spirit still “animates the true disciples… and enables them to speak,” “the Head… in his members.” Ellicott enters the necessary caveat: the words are “strong,” human thought seeming “utterly suppressed,” but it would be “beside the drift” of the discourse to make this promise of special aid “the groundwork of a theory of inspiration” covering the written records.

The witnesses dwell, too, on the tenderness of “your Father.” Clarke notes the title “is added to excite and increase their confidence in God” — the same Spirit who is called the Comforter, the Spirit of Truth, and the Spirit of God being here named “the Spirit of your Father” (so the stray Cyril fragment, ranging the titles of the one Spirit). The comfort, all agree, is that the poor and unlearned, set before kings, are not left to their own resources but have God himself undertaking their plea — “neither surprise, defect of talents, nor even ignorance… could hurt the cause of God” in such hands (Clarke).

The witnesses balance the strength of the promise against its proper bounds. On the one hand it is, as the fathers urge, the very Spirit who “forms and guides the tongues of believers” (Calvin), so that “it is I who speak” through them (Remigius); on the other, as Ellicott cautions, it would be “beside the drift” of the discourse to make a promise given for “moments of special danger” into “the groundwork of a theory of inspiration” covering all the written records. Gill’s careful phrasing holds the two together: they truly speak, yet “not so much they” as the Spirit, “the efficient cause” to their “instrument.” The added title “your Father,” all agree, is the heart of the comfort — the unlettered set before kings are not abandoned to their own resources, but have God himself, as a Father, undertaking their plea.
21:
On Matthew 10:21 — “the brother shall deliver up the brother to death… and children shall rise up against their parents” — the witnesses are struck by the unnatural cruelty that enmity to Christ can produce.

The Latin fathers feel the sharpness of betrayal by kin. Gregory observes that “wrongs which we suffer from strangers, pain us less than those… from men on whose affections we had counted,” adding to the bodily affliction “the pain of lost affection”; Jerome notes that “there is… no true affection between those whose faith is different.” The Gloss marks the structure — “having placed the comfort first, He adds the more alarming perils.” Chrysostom carries the thought into the next clause (“ye shall be hated of all men”), with its consolation, “for my name’s sake,” and its demand of endurance.

The grammatical-historical and Reformed commentators measure the depth of human depravity here disclosed. Barnes finds the prophecy scarcely credible but for history: only “dreadful opposition to God” could make parents and children “deliver up… to torture, racks, and flames” their own; Clarke cries “O shame to men!” that any “think they did God service” in such murder. Ellicott notes the words “reproduce almost verbally the prophecy of Micah 7:6,” there followed by the prophet’s resolve to “wait for the God of my salvation” — answering to the endurance of the next verse. Calvin adds the careful balance that such division “does not happen to none but believers”: a father may rightly pursue an apostate son “by a zeal that is holy” (Deuteronomy 13:9), so that “if our affections were properly regulated, there would be no other cause of just hatred among us” — the monstrous thing being hatred of the ministers of “so lovely a doctrine.”

The witnesses let the verse measure the depth of human enmity to the gospel. Barnes confesses that “were there no evidence that this had been done, it could scarcely be credible,” so strong are the ties of blood; yet history shows parents and children delivering up their own “to torture, racks, and flames” — proof of “the malignity of the human heart by nature against religion.” The echo of Micah 7:6 sets the saying in the prophets’ long lament over a corrupt age, answered there by the resolve to “wait for the God of my salvation.” Calvin’s careful distinction guards the truth: such division is “a thing so monstrous, and so contrary to nature,” that it might “greatly distress the minds of simple men” — which is just why Christ “foretells that it will actually take place,” that none be overthrown when it comes.
22:
On Matthew 10:22 — “ye shall be hated of all men for my name’s sake: but he that endureth to the end shall be saved” — the witnesses read both the breadth of the hatred and the crown of perseverance.

The grammatical-historical commentators qualify “all men.” Gill and Barnes take it as “all kinds of men” or the greater part, not without exception, since some would be converted and honour them; the hatred is “not on account of any… evil” in them but “purely… for” Christ’s name. Robertson notes the periphrastic future — the hatred “will go on through the ages” — and that “the name” stands for the person (Acts 5:41). Ellicott marks the precision of the prophecy: “from the days of Stephen to… the last martyr under Diocletian it was always as a Christian and for the name of Christ” that men suffered — Tertullian’s word, “the battle is about a Name” (compare 1 Peter 4:16).

On “he that endureth to the end,” the schools converge. Chrysostom warns that “many are hot… in the beginning, but afterwards grow cool”; Christ “looks at the end,” and perseverance “was their work,” lest any think he “wrought all things” in them. Remigius notes “the reward is not for those that begin, but for those that bring to an end,” and that “the end” may mean not destruction but perfection — “Christ is the end of the Law” (Romans 10:4) — so “endure to the end” is to endure “in Christ”; Augustine: “to endure in Christ, is to abide in His faith which worketh by love.” Clarke alone hears a reference to surviving “the destruction that shall fall” on Jerusalem, while Calvin makes the promise sheer encouragement: those “certain of victory” should not “hesitate to abide by the cause of Christ to the very last.”

The witnesses dwell on the great closing word, “he that endureth to the end.” Henry draws three comforts from it: that “there will be an end of these troubles… they will not last always” (Luke 22:37); that while they last “they may be endured,” the sufferers “borne up… in everlasting arms,” the strength “according to the day” (1 Corinthians 10:13); and that “salvation will be the eternal recompence.” The promise, he notes, is not only an “encouragement to us to endure, but an engagement to endure to the end,” for those who “fall away… have run in vain.” Be faithful unto death, and “then thou shalt have the crown of life” (Revelation 2:10) — the hatred of all men outweighed by the prospect of the crown that perseverance alone secures.
23:
On Matthew 10:23 — “when they persecute you in this city, flee ye into another… ye shall not have gone over the cities of Israel, till the Son of man be come” — the witnesses agree that flight is lawful and divide sharply over the coming of the Son of man.

On flight the schools converge. Chrysostom and the younger Chrysostom note that Christ “does not bid them presumptuously… offer themselves for persecution, but to fly,” adapting his words to a “beginning.” Augustine guards it: not from inability to protect them, but to instruct “man’s weakness” not to “tempt God” — yet a pastor must not desert a flock under common danger. Remigius distinguishes: endurance “specially belongs to the Apostles… men of fortitude,” while leave to flee suits “the weak in the faith”; Ellicott and Calvin agree martyrdom is not to be “courted” but danger used wisely — the “wisdom of the serpent” — Polycarp and Cyprian the great examples.

On “till the Son of man be come” the witnesses offer at least four readings. Lightfoot and (allowing it) Calvin refer it to the resurrection and the power of the Spirit, by which Christ’s reign would shine with such lustre that the apostles would “discern that glory… they had hitherto been unable to discover” (compare Romans 1:4; Acts 3:19). Barnes, with Rosenmuller, takes it of “the destruction of Jerusalem… about thirty years after” (Matthew 24:30). Hilary reads it of the second coming — “after the fulness of the Gentiles is brought in,” the remnant of Israel called “in Christ’s future coming to glory” — and Ellicott, citing Daniel 7:13, leans the same way, candidly owning the difficulty and the limits of what was revealed (Mark 13:32). Clarke even floats an alternative rendering — “ye shall not have instructed… the cities” — while Robertson lists the views without choosing, refusing the charge that “Jesus was mistaken.”

The witnesses make this the meeting-place of prudence and providence. Flight is no cowardice but obedience — “it is no inglorious thing for Christ’s soldiers to quit their ground, provided they do not quit their colours” (Henry); they “may go out of the way of danger, though they must not go out of the way of duty,” and only when no “sinful, unlawful means” are used, “for then it is not a door of God’s opening.” Christ’s care provides “places of retreat,” ordering it that “persecution rages not in all places at the same time.” Augustine’s pastoral rule tempers it: a shepherd must not desert a flock under common danger, but “sometimes… by his absence he will quell the rage of enemies.” Whatever the disputed “coming” means, all agree it “sufficed for their consolation, simply to see Him” — their working time short and their Lord soon at hand.
24:
On Matthew 10:24 — “the disciple is not above his master, nor the servant above his lord” — the witnesses read the proverb as comfort drawn from Christ’s own ill-usage.

The eastern and Latin fathers make it a consolation against the loss of good name. Chrysostom notes that since the disciples would suffer “loss of character, which to many is the most grievous of all calamities,” Christ consoles them “from His own example”; he says not “slaves” but “those of his household,” “to shew how dear they were to Him” (compare John 15:15). Hilary calls it “this solace” — that “we should embrace it as our glory when we are made like to our Lord in suffering.” Remigius and the Gloss draw the same lesson: the disciples “will not seek worldly honours… while you see me pursuing the redemption of mankind through mocking and contumely,” for the Lord does what he wills and teaches what is profitable.

The grammatical-historical and Puritan commentators set the saying in its wider Gospel use. The same proverb appears where Christ urges the lowliest service — washing one another’s feet (John 13:16) — and again as a reason not to stumble at suffering (John 15:20). Henry unfolds it: Christ is “our teaching Master… our ruling master… Master of the house” with “a despotic power in the church, which is his family”; and since our Lord “met with very hard usage from the world,” the consideration “should engage us to expect and prepare for the like, and to bear it patiently” — not thinking it strange “if they who hated him hate his followers, for his sake.”

The witnesses turn the proverb wholly to comfort. Christ consoles his servants “from His own example” — “a comfort to which no other can be compared” (Chrysostom) — against the loss of good name, “which to many is the most grievous of all calamities.” The tenderness is marked in his words: not “slaves” but “those of his household,” even his “friends” (John 15:15). Henry presses the threefold lesson — that since the disciple is not above his Master, the hard usage Christ met should make us “expect and prepare for the like,” “not think it strange” to be hated as he was hated, and remember that those “shortly to be made like him in glory” are “now made like him in sufferings.” The wonder is double — men’s wickedness in so abusing Christ, and Christ’s patience in suffering himself to be abused.
25:
On Matthew 10:25 — “if they have called the master of the house Beelzebub, how much more shall they call them of his household?” — the witnesses dwell on the foulness of the slander and the dignity of sharing it.

The Latin fathers identify the name. Jerome explains Beelzebub as the idol of Ekron, “the god of flies” (2 Kings 1:3) — “Bel” signifying “idol,” “zebub” a “fly” — so that “the Prince of the daemons He calls by the name of the foulest of idols.” Remigius and the Gloss draw out the comfort: if the master of the house is so reviled, his household must not expect better, nor “be indignant that ye suffer things, which I also suffer.” Chrysostom (on the previous verse) had noted that Christ “expresses the very words of railing, for they had called Him Beelzebub.”

The Puritan Henry presses the astonishing inversion. The blackest of the names given Christ’s servants is “Beelzebub, the name of the prince of the devils”: thus “Satan’s sworn enemies are represented as his friends” — “the apostles, who pulled down the devil’s kingdom, were called devils” — while “Satan’s sworn servants would be thought to be his enemies,” for “those that paint him on others’ clothes have him reigning in their own hearts.” Persecutors must first “dress up… in bear-skins whom they thus bait,” to make them “odious to all mankind”; yet, Henry adds, “there is a day coming, when… that which is hid will be brought to light” (Matthew 10:26). The wonder is double — “the wickedness of men who thus abused Christ, or the patience of Christ, who suffered himself to be thus abused.”

The witnesses make the verse a mirror of the world’s perverse judgment. The slander is the worst conceivable — the foulest of idols, the “god of flies” (2 Kings 1:3), the prince of demons — flung at the Lord himself, that he might be made “odious to all mankind” (Henry). Yet the slander recoils: “Satan’s sworn enemies are represented as his friends” while his true servants “would be thought to be his enemies,” so that “those that paint him on others’ clothes have him reigning in their own hearts.” The household that bears the Master’s name must look for the Master’s reproach — “how much more shall they call them of his household” — and bear it without indignation, since they suffer only what he suffered first. The day is coming, the witnesses add, “when that which is hid will be brought to light” (Matthew 10:26).
26:
On Matthew 10:26 — “fear them not therefore: for there is nothing covered, that shall not be revealed” — the witnesses read both a command against fear and a double promise of disclosure.

The Puritan Henry unfolds the “revealing” two ways. First, of the gospel itself: “the truths which are now, as mysteries, hid… shall all be made known, to all nations” (Acts 2:11) — “a great encouragement to those who are doing Christ’s work, that it is a work which shall certainly be done.” Second, of “the clearing up of the innocency” of Christ’s slandered servants, called Beelzebub: though their excellency be “now covered, they shall be revealed,” if not in this world then “at the great day” (1 Corinthians 4:5) — “a resurrection of names as well as of bodies.”

The grammatical-historical commentators ground the command against fear in the limited power of the enemy, looking ahead to the next verses. Henry, expounding the whole, gives the reason and the remedy: the persecutors “can but kill the body… not… the soul,” which is “out of the reach of their power” (Romans 8:35); the remedy is “to fear God… able to destroy both soul and body in hell,” for “the fear of God… will be a sovereign antidote against the fear of man” (Acts 4:19). The disclosure that is coming, all the voices agree, both emboldens the witness — since the truth he carries “shall certainly be” published — and steadies him under reproach, since his hidden integrity will at last “shine forth as the sun.”

The witnesses make the command against fear rest on the certainty of disclosure. Henry’s two readings reinforce each other: the gospel hid “as mysteries” shall be “made known to all nations” (Acts 2:11), “a plough which God will speed”; and the slandered servants’ “innocency and excellency,” now “covered,” shall “shine forth as the light” — if not here, then “at the great day,” in “a resurrection of names as well as of bodies” (1 Corinthians 4:5). Thus the minister is to “faithfully reveal his truths, and then leave it to him, in due time, to reveal their integrity.” The fear of those who “can but kill the body” is groundless because “the fear of man brings a snare” that disturbs peace and draws into sin — a snare to be “watched, and striven, and prayed against” in the confidence that nothing finally stays hidden.
27:
On Matthew 10:27 — “what I tell you in darkness, that speak ye in light: and what ye hear in the ear, that preach ye upon the housetops” — the witnesses read a charge to publish the gospel openly, whatever the hazard.

The Puritan Henry catches the force: “whatever hazards you run, go on with your work, publishing… the everlasting gospel to all the world; that is your business.” The enemies’ design “is not merely to destroy you, but to suppress that” — therefore “whatever be the consequence, publish that.” What the apostles delivered “is the same that they received from Jesus Christ” (Hebrews 2:3) — “that, all that, and nothing but that.” The private instruction, given “in the ear, in corners, in parables” and especially the things of the kingdom whispered after the resurrection (Acts 1:3), was to be delivered “in the light, and upon the housetops,” for the gospel is what “all are concerned in” (Proverbs 1:20).

The grammatical-historical commentators read the contrast of darkness and light as the contrast between Christ’s comparatively private teaching of the Twelve and the open proclamation now committed to them — with the eastern image of the flat housetop, the very place from which, as Henry notes, “the first indication of the reception of the Gentiles… was” given (Acts 10:9). There is, all agree, “no part of Christ’s gospel that needs… to be concealed”; “the whole counsel of God must be revealed” (Acts 20:27), plainly and fully delivered to every mixed multitude — “he that hath ears to hear, let him hear.”

The witnesses gather the verse as a charge of holy boldness. The contrast of darkness and light, Henry explains, is not of any secret doctrine but of manner: the things Christ taught the Twelve privately — “in the ear, in corners, in parables” — and the kingdom-truths whispered after the resurrection (Acts 1:3) were to be “delivered… publicly… upon the house-tops,” for the gospel is what “all are concerned in” (Proverbs 8:2). What they preach is exactly “what he told them — that, all that, and nothing but that” (Hebrews 2:3); “there is no part of Christ’s gospel that needs… to be concealed,” the “whole counsel of God” to be revealed (Acts 20:27). The enemies’ design being to suppress the message, the disciple’s answer is to publish it the more openly — “he that hath ears to hear, let him hear.”
28:
On Matthew 10:28 — “fear not them which kill the body, but are not able to kill the soul: but rather fear him which is able to destroy both soul and body in hell” — the witnesses read the great reason and remedy against the fear of man.

The Puritan Henry gives the fullest exposition. The persecutors’ power is “limited”: “they kill the body… the utmost their rage can extend to… they are not able to kill the soul.” By this, he argues, it appears the soul does not “fall asleep at death,” for then “the killing of the body would be the killing of the soul too”; the soul is killed only when “separated from God and his love,” which is “out of the reach of their power” (Romans 8:35). The remedy is “to fear God”: hell is “the destruction both of soul and body… the ruin of the whole man,” a destruction “from his glorious power” (2 Thessalonians 1:9), so that God is “to be feared, even by the best saints,” the God of Abraham being called “the Fear of Isaac” (Genesis 31:42).

The grammatical-historical commentators agree the verse turns the holy fear of God into the cure of the unholy fear of man: “they who truly fear God, need not fear man,” and “it is better to fall under the frowns of all the world, than under God’s frowns” (Isaiah 51:12). The whole counsel is that the disciple, weighing the body against the soul and man’s power against God’s, will find the racks and gibbets, terrible as they are, shrink before the reverence due to him who alone holds the everlasting issue — obeying “God rather than men” (Acts 4:19).

The witnesses make this the crown of the chapter’s counsels against fear. The reasoning, Henry shows, proves much by the way: that the soul does not “fall asleep at death,” for then “the killing of the body would be the killing of the soul too”; that the soul is “killed” only when “separated from God and his love, which is its life” — a thing “out of the reach” of any persecutor (Romans 8:35). The remedy, the holy fear of God, is “a sovereign antidote against the fear of man,” for “they who truly fear God, need not fear man.” Even “the best saints in this world” are to fear him — “happy is the man that feareth always” — since “none knows the power of his anger” (Psalm 90:11). Racks and gibbets, terrible as they are, reach only the body; God alone holds “the well-being of both,” and is therefore to be feared above all.
29:
On Matthew 10:29 — “are not two sparrows sold for a farthing? and one of them shall not fall on the ground without your Father” — the witnesses read the doctrine of a particular providence reaching to the least of creatures.

The Latin and eastern fathers argue from the less to the greater. Chrysostom says the saying is meant “to shew His diligent knowledge, and great carefulness over us”; Jerome: “if these little creations fall not without God’s… providence… you who are immortal ought not to fear that you live without His providence.” Augustine takes occasion to discuss the resurrection of the body — the numbered hairs meaning that “nothing of our body shall be lost,” though the shorn hair need not return to its old place. The grammatical-historical commentators fix the coin and the proverb: the assarion is “the tenth part of the denarius” (Ellicott), and Clarke cites the rabbinic tale of the bird that escapes or is snared only as “Heaven” decrees — “even a bird is not taken without Heaven… how much less the life of man.”

The Reformed Calvin draws the sharpest doctrine, contrasting true providence with the philosophers’ “contingence.” Christ teaches that “each of the creatures in particular is under his hand… so that nothing is left to chance” — the will of God set against the notion of “an accident which comes of its own accord.” Yet, Calvin adds, this is no Stoic fate; the believer traces “every event” to God’s will (Ephesians 1:11), and contemplates providence “not as curious… persons,” but as “a ground of confidence and excitement to prayer.” Wesley sums the comfort: if God cares for “the most inconsiderable creatures, how much more will he take care of you.” The misattached Cyril fragment, marshalling the verse to prove the Father of Christ is the Maker of the world, sits beside it as a stray.

The witnesses press the comfort of providence to its practical point. The doctrine is given, Calvin insists, “not to encourage trivial speculations, but to instruct us to depend on the fatherly care of God” over “these frail bodies.” Wesley draws the chain plainly: if God’s care “extends to the very smallest things… how much more will he take care of you” — and that “not only in this life, but in the other also.” The hairs of the head, numbered, show “a care unspeakable that nothing of ours is hid from God” (Jerome); not that God literally “reckons our hairs,” but that nothing is too small for his knowledge. Against the enemy who, “like subtle fowlers,” lays snares, the disciple is safe: the bird falls “not… without the direction of providence,” and the persecutors “have no power… but what is given them from above” (Psalm 124:7).
30:
On Matthew 10:30 — “but the very hairs of your head are all numbered” — the witnesses take the proverb as the high-water mark of God’s minute care for his people.

The grammatical-historical commentators agree it is “a proverbial expression” (Gill) for the “perfect knowledge” and “exquisite care” God exercises over his creatures, and especially his children and ministers. Barnes presses the logic of the figure: each hair “has exercised the care and attention of God”; he has “fixed the number,” and though it is “of small importance,” he does not think it “beneath him to determine how few, or how many” — “he will, therefore, take care of you.” Clarke marks the difference between “that general providence which extends to all things” and the “particular providence, which fits and directs all things to the design of their salvation,” causing all to “co-operate for their… eternal good” (Romans 8:28).

Ellicott guards against pressing the figure too far: the “apparent hyperbole” is “but the natural expression of the thought that even the incidents of life that seem most trivial are… working together for good to those that love God.” The disciples “are not at any moment of their lives to think that they are uncared for by their Father.” The numbering of the hairs, all the voices agree, is not a matter for “curious enquiry” (so Henry on the same words) but for “encouragement to live in a continual dependence upon God’s providential care” — a care so exact that, if God numbers their hairs, “much more does he number their heads, and take care of their lives… their souls,” taking “more care of them than they do of themselves.”

The witnesses make this hyperbole the very pledge of fatherly tenderness. If God keeps account of a thing so slight and so often lost as the hairs of the head — which men “never miss” though they “number their money, and goods, and cattle” — then “much more does he number their heads, and take care of their lives, their comforts, their souls” (Henry). It “intimates that God takes more care of them than they do of themselves,” so that “not a hair of their head shall perish” without “a valuable consideration” (Luke 21:18). This minute providence, all agree, is not for “curious enquiry” but for “encouragement to live in a continual dependence” — and it is exercised “without disparagement to the infinite glory, or disturbance to the infinite rest, of the Eternal Mind.” So precious to God, the witnesses conclude, are his saints, “and their lives and deaths.”
31:
On Matthew 10:31 — “fear ye not therefore, ye are of more value than many sparrows” — the witnesses gather the conclusion of the providence argument: the disciples need fear neither men nor neglect.

The grammatical-historical commentators draw out the comfort. Gill reasons from the lesser to the greater: if providence “reaches to the meanest of creatures, sparrows, and to… the single hair of a man’s head; much more must it regard the lives of men,” and still more “such useful lives” as the disciples’, called to preach “the everlasting Gospel.” Robertson notes simply the ablative of comparison — “ye differ” from, are of more value than, “many sparrows.” Clarke makes the value the value of a soul: “none can estimate the value of a soul, for which Christ has given his blood and life,” and he rebukes the “cold and meagre… shallow deistical saying” that God “sees with equal eye… a Hero perish, or a Sparrow fall,” as if a sparrow were “of as much worth… as an immortal soul.”

The Reformed Calvin notes the double ground of the disciples’ worth. The preeminence of men over the lower creatures “is true in general of all men, for they were created for their advantage” (Psalm 8:6); but this discourse “relates peculiarly to the sons of God, who possess a far higher right than what they derive from creation” — a rank arising “solely from the undeserved kindness of God.” The fear of man, all the voices agree, is silenced not by any change in the danger but by the certainty that the God who counts the sparrows and numbers the hairs holds the disciple’s life and soul in his particular and fatherly care.

The witnesses make the conclusion turn the whole providence-argument into courage. “There is enough in the doctrine of God’s providence to silence all the fears of God’s people” (Henry): if the sparrow and the hair are not beneath his care, the disciple, worth “more than many sparrows,” is surely not. All men are of more value than the creatures, “for the other creatures were made for man” (Psalm 8:6); “much more the disciples of Jesus Christ, who are the excellent ones of the earth, however contemned.” Clarke fixes the worth at its true height — the soul “for which Christ has given his blood and life” — and bids the disciple “have confidence in his goodness,” since “he who so dearly purchased thee will… preserve and save thee.” The fear of man, on every reading, is dissolved not by safety but by the certainty of a Father’s particular love.
32:
On Matthew 10:32 — “whosoever therefore shall confess me before men, him will I confess also before my Father” — the witnesses read the necessity of open confession and the glory of being owned by Christ.

The eastern and Latin fathers note the grammar and the grace. Chrysostom observes the Greek is not “shall confess me” but “shall confess in me,” “shewing that it is not by your own strength but by grace from above” that you confess; and that the reward is “manifold more than the good done.” Hilary: “in what measure we have borne witness to Him upon earth, in the same shall we have Him to bear witness to us in heaven.” Remigius and Raban ground it in Romans 10:10 — “with the mouth confession is made unto salvation” — so that none may “suppose that he could be saved without confession of the mouth.”

The grammatical-historical and Reformed commentators stress the breadth and the cost of confession. It means, says Barnes, “to acknowledge… in every proper manner… in every circumstance of the life, and before all men”; Clarke: “there must be a firm, manly, and public profession of Christ before men,” for the man who says “I will keep my religion to myself… will renounce” him before God.
