Robertson notes the Aramaic idiom “confess in me,” “indicating a sense of unity with Christ.” Ellicott and Calvin mark that the saying reaches “beyond the apostles… to all who should receive their witness” (compare Luke 12:8); Calvin makes confession “a main part of divine worship,” for if earthly princes call subjects to arms, “why should not believers maintain… the glory of their heavenly King?” — weighing the base refusal of our testimony against the matchless honour of Christ’s testimony to us before God and the angels.

The witnesses weigh the great exchange the verse proposes. Calvin sets the contrast plainly: “if we draw a comparison between ourselves and the Son of God, how base is it to refuse our testimony to him, when… he offers his testimony to us by way of reward”; and how much more valuable is what Christ promises — acknowledgment before God and the angels — “than that which he requires.” Even where men are “unbelieving and rebellious,” the testimony delivered to them “is estimated by Christ as if it had been made in the presence of God.” The fathers add that the reward is “manifold more than the good done” (Chrysostom), and the observance of this precept “drew many to the Apostles.” A fuller confession, Calvin grants, is “demanded from teachers than from persons in a private station,” yet “there is no believer whom the Son of God does not require to be his witness.”
33:
On Matthew 10:33 — “but whosoever shall deny me before men, him will I also deny before my Father” — the witnesses read the solemn counterpart of the promise, a law of fearful symmetry.

The grammatical-historical commentators define the denial and its dread issue. Gill takes it as to “deny that he is a disciple… act contrary to him, deliver things repugnant to his mind” — in a master a heinous crime. Clarke widens it: “whosoever prefers his worldly interest to his duty to God… and prefers the friendship of men to the approbation of God” denies Christ; and to be “renounced by Christ is to have him neither for a Mediator nor Saviour” — to stand before God’s tribunal “without… Christ for our Advocate, and… as our Judge, and a witness against us.” Robertson marks the grammar: the accusative “deny me” is the “complete breach,” “saying ‘no’ to Christ” — “a solemn law, not a mere social breach… public and final.”

Ellicott sets the verse under the head of “all other eternal laws”: the blessing on those who fulfil the condition “has its counterpart of woe on those who do not.” To “deny Christ on earth by word or deed, to live as if His work were nothing to us, must lead to His denying us in the last great day.” The witnesses are careful, with Wesley, to note that the denial here meant is one “persisted in,” just as the confession that wins the promise is “the real and constant language of faith and love” — not a single lapse, but a settled repudiation that Christ will at last requite in kind, blotting such names “out of the book of life” (so Calvin on the parallel).

The witnesses dwell on the terror of the denial that is requited in kind. To deny Christ, Adam Clarke writes, and so “to appear before the tribunal of God without… Christ for our Advocate, and… as our Judge, and a witness against us — how can a man think of this and not die with horror!” The law is “solemn,… public and final” (Robertson), the cleavage by Christ of the man who repudiates him. Yet the witnesses temper it with the same care as the confession: it is the denial “persisted in,” the settled preferring of “worldly interest” and “the friendship of men to the approbation of God,” that Christ will disown — blotting such “out of the book of life” (Calvin on the parallel). The fearful symmetry, then, is also a summons: let none, by a cowardly silence, forfeit the Advocate they will most need in the great day.
34:
On Matthew 10:34 — “think not that I am come to send peace on earth: I came not to send peace, but a sword” — the witnesses resolve the paradox of the Prince of Peace who brings division.

The eastern and Latin fathers explain the “good discord.” Chrysostom asks how this agrees with “Peace be to this house” and the angels’ song (Luke 2:14), and answers that “the most perfect peace” comes “when that which is diseased is lopped off”: as the physician saves the body by amputation, and Babel’s “evil peace” was ended by “good discord,” so “concord is not in every case good, for there is honour among thieves.” The war “is not of His setting… but of the plots of the world.” Jerome roots the words in Micah 7:6 — “this holy war… that an unholy peace might be broken through”; Hilary and Augustine read the household division mystically, of the new man severed by “the sword of the Spirit” (Ephesians 6:17) from his old nature, or of the Church against the Synagogue.

The grammatical-historical commentators take it of the effect, not the design. Barnes: Christ “did not… mean… that the object of his coming was to produce discord” (Isaiah 9:6) but that “such would be one of the effects” — “the wickedness of men, and not the religion of the gospel,” the cause. Robertson calls it “a bold and dramatic climax… no peace at any price”; Ellicott connects it with the sword foretold to pierce Mary’s soul (Luke 2:35). Lightfoot and Clarke add a striking literal reading: the words “primarily point out… those horrid… civil wars of the Jews,” the Roman sword sent “to cut off a disobedient… nation” whose iniquity was full.

The witnesses keep the paradox from misuse. Christ is “our peace” (Ephesians 2:14) and “came to be the one great Peacemaker” (Ellicott); the sword is “the foreseen consequence of His work,” freely accepted “for the sake of the greater good that lies beyond.” Chrysostom drives home that “concord is not in every case good” — “there is honour among thieves” — and that the “good discord” which severs the believer from a conspiracy of evil is the only road by which “heaven should be joined to earth.” He notes the proof of Christ’s “very great power”: that the disciples, hearing of so dreadful a warfare — sharper than civil war, dividing “the nearest and dearest kindred” — “yet undertook the mission, and brought over others.” The sword is not Christ’s purpose but the world’s temper; he names it plainly only that his followers, forewarned, might “not fall off in time of trial.”
35:
On Matthew 10:35 — “for I am come to set a man at variance against his father… and the daughter in law against her mother in law” — the witnesses explain the sword of the previous verse as the division the gospel provokes within the closest ties.

The grammatical-historical commentators trace the words to Micah 7:6. Gill notes Christ here “opens and explains what he means by the sword, intestine divisions, domestic broils, family differences.” Clarke locates the antagonism precisely: “the spirit of Christ can have no union with the spirit of the world… it is the spirits… that are in opposition, and not the persons.” Robertson, rendering dichasai “divide in two,” notes that Jesus uses Micah “to describe the rottenness of the age,” for “family ties… cannot stand in the way of loyalty to Christ”; the “daughter in law” is literally the young bride, perhaps living with her husband’s mother. Ellicott wonders whether the choice of these relationships hints at “some personal application” — Zebedee’s sons, Peter’s household, or the unbelieving brethren of the Lord.

The Reformed Calvin keeps the cause firmly on human malice. “Wars and tumults arise, contrary to the nature of the Gospel, through the fault of wicked men”; what Malachi 4:6 says of John the Baptist — that he should turn the hearts of fathers to children — “applies to all the ministers of Christ,” yet “through the malice of wicked men” those once united “separate into opposite parties.” Calvin guards the saying from abuse: Christ “does not mean that this will uniformly take place,” as if none could be a good disciple “without forsaking parents, children, and wives”; rather “every lawful bond of union is confirmed by unity of faith,” and he only warns that, when such division does come, his followers “must not be alarmed.”

The witnesses keep the cause of the division squarely on human sin, not the gospel. Christ “sets a man at variance” only in the sense that his truth, faithfully held, exposes the enmity already latent in the unconverted heart: the spirit of the world “can have no union with the spirit of Christ” (Clarke), so that “even a father, while unconverted, will oppose a godly child.” The instanced relationships — father and son, mother and daughter, the bride and her husband’s mother — are chosen, the witnesses note, because they are the closest, where love should leave “little room… for envy.” Calvin guards the saying from the “fretful” notion that discipleship requires “forsaking parents, children, and wives”: “every lawful bond of union is confirmed by unity of faith.” The tragedy Robertson names — “to see a father or mother step between the child and Christ” — is the rottenness of the age, not the fault of the kingdom.
36:
On Matthew 10:36 — “and a man’s foes shall be they of his own household” — the witnesses note that the bitterest opposition rises from within the home, and that the very saying marks Jesus as the Messiah.

The grammatical-historical commentators ground the words in Micah 7:6 and in Jewish expectation. Gill notes that “the times of the Gospel are set forth in the same dismal… characters” as the prophet’s own age, “his children, and his servants… daily fed at his table,” turned enemies. Clarke is most striking here: he cites the rabbis’ own sayings about the days of Messiah — “the son shall insult the father, the daughter rebel against her mother, the daughter-in-law against her mother-in-law… each man shall have his own household for his enemies” (Sotah; Sanhedrin) — and argues that by these “our Lord shows them that he was the Messiah,” for “all these things literally took place shortly after their… rejection of Christ,” in the desolations Josephus records.

The witnesses dwell, with Clarke (citing Quesnel), on how household enmity works both ways and in subtler forms: “the father is the enemy of his son” through “a bad education… a cruel indulgence”; the son his father’s, when he is “the occasion of his doing injustice” to heap up an estate; the mother and daughter mutual foes through vanity and idolatrous love; the master and servant, when neither “takes care of the other’s salvation.” Wesley keeps the application narrow and sharp — “the foes of a man that loves and follows me” (Micah 7:6). The verse, on every reading, prepares the disciple for the costliest trial: that enmity to Christ can arm even those bound by “the strongest bonds of love and duty.”

The witnesses let the verse close the catalogue of sufferings on its most piercing note — that the foe is “they of his own household.” The peculiar grief of it, Henry observes elsewhere in this discourse, is that such wrongs come from those “from whom we might expect protection and sympathy.” Clarke’s gathering of the rabbinic sayings makes the verse a quiet apologetic: the Jews’ own traditions foretold that “in the age in which the Messiah shall come… the son shall insult the father… and the man… shall have his own household for his enemies” — and these things “literally took place shortly after their… rejection of Christ.” The Lord thus shows, by their own oracles, “that he was the Messiah.” Quesnel’s sobering note widens the application: father and son, mother and daughter, master and servant become “foes” whenever one “takes no care of the other’s salvation” — the deepest enmity being the neglect of a soul.
37:
On Matthew 10:37 — “he that loveth father or mother more than me is not worthy of me” — the witnesses are careful that Christ neither abolishes natural affection nor permits it to rival himself.

The Latin and eastern fathers insist on the right ordering of love. Jerome cites the Song — “order love in me” (Song of Solomon 2:4) — “after God love thy father… but if… the love of parents… comes into competition with the love of God… hatred of our kindred becomes then love to God”; the Lord “forbids not to love… but adds emphatically, ‘more than me.’” Chrysostom harmonizes it with the duty to obey parents (only “in such things as are not hurtful to our piety”), citing the Levites’ zeal (Deuteronomy 33:9), and presses the higher demand — that one must hate even his own life. The Gloss notes the ascending scale — love of Christ preferred first to parents, then to children.

The grammatical-historical and Reformed commentators read it as supreme love, not abolished affection. Barnes: “Christ must be loved supremely, or he is not loved at all.” Ellicott explains the harder word of Luke 14:26 (“hating father or mother”): “where two affections come into collision, the weaker must give way,” so that a man “must act as if he hated” those whom he still loves. Calvin agrees Christ “does not… enjoin us to lay aside human affections… but only desires that all the mutual love… should be so regulated as to assign the highest rank to piety” (Philippians 3:8). The stray Cyril fragment makes the same point from the other side — we must honour our earthly fathers too, since the Lord said not “he that loveth father or mother” but added “more than me.”

The witnesses guard the saying on both sides — against those who would make it abolish natural love, and against those who would let love rival Christ. It “forbids not to love parent or child,” Jerome insists, but “adds emphatically, more than me”; the order of love is the point — “after God love thy father… but… where both cannot be preserved,” the lesser yields. Chrysostom notes the divine wisdom in the method: Christ “uses those things which men’s affections are set upon as a means of bringing them to their duty,” even their love of life. Cyril of Jerusalem turns the same coin to its other face: “while honouring our heavenly Father let us honour also the fathers of our flesh” (Exodus 20:12), for the Lord said not simply “he that loveth father or mother” but “more than me” — obedience to parents being withheld only when they “are of a contrary mind to our Father in heaven.”
38:
On Matthew 10:38 — “and he that taketh not his cross, and followeth after me, is not worthy of me” — the witnesses read the first mention of the cross as the call to bear suffering and shame after Christ.

The grammatical-historical commentators explain the figure from Roman custom. Barnes, Clarke, and Robertson note that the condemned “should carry the cross on which they were to die to the place of execution” (compare John 19:17); to carry it “was burdensome… disgraceful… an addition to the punishment,” so that to “take up the cross” is to “endure whatever is burdensome, or trying, or… disgraceful, in following Christ” — not “making trouble for ourselves,” but “doing just what is required… let it produce whatever shame… it may” (Barnes). Robertson notes this is “the first mention of cross in Matthew,” the Jews familiar with crucifixion since the Maccabees, and that it is uncertain whether Jesus was “thinking of his own coming crucifixion,” though “possible, perhaps probable.” Ellicott agrees the words imply “a distinct prevision” (compare John 3:14), recalling to the disciples “the procession of robbers or rebels, each carrying the cross.”

The Latin fathers and Reformed draw out the fellowship in suffering. Gregory derives “cross” from torment, and names two ways of bearing it — “when we afflict the flesh by abstinence” or “in compassion for our neighbour” — but only when joined to “followeth me,” lest abstinence be for “ostentation.” Hilary makes the cross the place where “we suffer with Him, die with Him… and rise again with Him” (Galatians 5:24). Calvin reckons it the general law — “we cannot be reckoned his disciples unless we are prepared to endure many afflictions” — with the consolation that “in the cross we are the companions of Christ.”

The witnesses read the cross here as the great test of discipleship and its hidden mercy. Chrysostom marks the wisdom of the timing: Christ “had as yet said nothing… respecting his own sufferings, but instructs them… in these things, that they may the more readily receive His words concerning His passion.” The demand seems burdensome, so he “proceeds to shew their great use and benefit” in the verse that follows — that to lose life for his sake is to find it. Each disciple, Gill notes, “has his own cross, his own particular afflictions, appointed by God,” to be “cheerfully” taken up, after the pattern of the Lord who bore his own to Calvary. The consolation, Calvin adds, is decisive: while “to those who are out of Christ the cross is accursed,” to his followers “in the cross we are the companions of Christ” — a fellowship that “will speedily… allay all its bitterness.”
39:
On Matthew 10:39 — “he that findeth his life shall lose it: and he that loseth his life for my sake shall find it” — the witnesses unfold the great paradox on the double sense of “life.”

The grammatical-historical commentators turn on the two meanings. Barnes states it plainly: “he that is anxious to save his temporal life… shall lose eternal life… he that is willing to… lose his comfort and life here, for my sake, shall find life everlasting.” Ellicott notes “life” is the same word rendered “soul” in Matthew 10:28, the point lying “in the contrast between the two senses”: “to gain the lower now is to lose the higher hereafter.” Robertson observes the paradox “appears in four forms” across the Gospels (compare John 12:25), “one of the profound sayings of Christ that he repeated many times.” Clarke fixes it with a martyr’s tale — Archbishop Cranmer, who “to save his life… recanted, and was, notwithstanding, burnt” — and Juvenal’s noble line, that one should “disdain to barter innocence for life.”

The Latin fathers and Reformed stress the motive and the recompense. Remigius takes “life” here as “this present state of being”: he who so loves “this light, its joys and pleasures” as always to keep them “shall lose” them and “prepare his soul for eternal damnation.” Raban joins it to the cross of the previous verse — “he who seeks an immortal life, does not hesitate to lose his life.” Calvin notes Christ “confirms” the hard doctrine “in two ways”: the over-cautious, thinking their life well defended (Psalm 49:18), “will be disappointed,” for they will die at last; while those who “surrender themselves to die” for Christ find their life “pass into a better life.”

The witnesses prize the saying as one of Christ’s deepest and most repeated. Its force, all agree, lies in the contrast of the two lives: to “find” the lower — to keep one’s comfort and safety “by a sinful compliance… and by a denial of Christ” (Gill) — is to “lose” the higher in “an eternal death,” while to lose the lower “for Christ’s sake” is to find it “to his unspeakable advantage, an eternal life.” Calvin meets the strong attachment to life with strong promise and threat alike: the over-cautious, who think their life “very well defended,” will be “disappointed,” for they must die at last; the believer who surrenders sees his life “pass into a better.” Yet the motive must be right — Christ “expressly states the reason why we ought to expose ourselves to death,” lest any “heedlessly lay down” life “for the sake of ambition or of madness.”
40:
On Matthew 10:40 — “he that receiveth you receiveth me; and he that receiveth me receiveth him that sent me” — the witnesses read the discourse closing in promise: Christ identifies himself, and the Father, with his messengers.

The Latin and eastern fathers mark the dignity and the office. Chrysostom asks “what honour to be compared to this” — that in receiving the apostles one entertains “the Father and the Son”; and notes that the reward attaches not to greatness but to the title, “in the name of a prophet.” Hilary finds here Christ’s “Mediator’s office”: “Christ dwells in them, and God in Christ,” so that “to have received the Apostles is no other than to have received God.” Jerome meets a hidden objection — what if the one received be a false prophet or a Judas? — answering that “it is not the person but the office” that matters, and “the entertainer loses not his reward, though he whom he entertains be unworthy.”

The grammatical-historical and Reformed commentators draw out the comfort and the law of representation. Barnes notes the three illustrations teach “substantially the same thing”: to receive Christ’s servant “as a prophet” shows approval of his cause. Gill makes it a consolation against the chapter’s catalogue of persecutions — some “would embrace the Gospel… and receive them kindly.” Ellicott: as Christ “was sent by the Father” (John 20:21; Hebrews 3:1), so the apostles are his representatives, “honour… shown to them… shown also to Himself.” Calvin marks how dearly Christ loves them, placing “to his own account the kind offices” done them (compare Matthew 25:40).

The witnesses make this closing promise the warm answer to the chapter’s long catalogue of perils. After so much of hatred, scourging, and the sword, Gill notes, the disciples might fear “that there would be none that would receive them”; Christ assures them otherwise — some “would embrace the Gospel… and receive them kindly into their houses.” And the honour of that welcome rises by stages to its height: to receive the apostle is to receive Christ, and to receive Christ is to receive “him that sent me.” The kindness done to a minister, Calvin notes, Christ “does not hesitate to reckon as done to himself” — showing “how dearly he loves them” — and “in such a sacrifice God the Father” takes pleasure. Jerome adds the freeing word that it is “not the person but the office” the host regards, so that he “loses not his reward, though he whom he entertains be unworthy.”
41:
On Matthew 10:41 — “he that receiveth a prophet in the name of a prophet shall receive a prophet’s reward” — the witnesses dwell on the motive of the welcome and the reward proportioned to it.

The grammatical-historical commentators stress the words “in the name of.” Gill explains “a prophet” here is “a preacher of the Gospel,” received not for kinship or learning or eloquence “but because he is a faithful minister.” Robertson glosses “in the name of a prophet” as “because he is a prophet” — received “from no ulterior motive” — and notes the idiom eis onoma. Ellicott finds in the distinction of prophet and righteous man “the higher inspiration of the prophet as a messenger of God,” perhaps implying that such inspiration “belonged to some, and not to all the Twelve,” while the rest were called “to set forth the pattern of a righteous life”; the reward and its time “belong to the future glory of the kingdom.” Clarke cites the rabbinic parallel — “he who receives a learned man… into his house, is the same as if he had received the Shekinah” — and adds that even if the teacher prove “worthless,” the host “shall not lose his reward,” having received him “for the sake of Christ.”

The Latin fathers and Reformed read the reward as fellowship in the prophet’s work. Gregory: he who supports the prophet, lacking bodily sustenance, “makes himself a free partaker in his righteousness,” for “if the body be not supported… the voice will fail.” Calvin commends “all… who truly worship God and love the gospel,” the reward being “the recompense which corresponds to the rank of the person” honoured — a “remarkable proof of the high estimation in which he holds… all his disciples.”

The witnesses dwell on the gracious breadth of the promise. The reward is sure not for the splendour of the guest but for the motive of the welcome — received “in the name of a prophet,” that is, “because he is a prophet,” not for kinship or learning or eloquence (Gill, Robertson). Gregory’s image makes the host a true partner: he who feeds the prophet, whose voice would else “fail” for want of bodily sustenance, “gives him strength for speaking,” and so “shall receive the prophet’s reward.” Ellicott opens the gate wide: the words admit “not only those whose gifts… command the admiration of mankind, but all those who show in action that they are in sympathy with the work.” Clarke’s rabbinic parallel crowns it — to receive a teacher of God is “the same as if he had received the Shekinah” — and even should the teacher prove worthless, the host who received him “for the sake of Christ” loses nothing.
42:
On Matthew 10:42 — “whosoever shall give to drink unto one of these little ones a cup of cold water only in the name of a disciple… he shall in no wise lose his reward” — the witnesses close the discourse on the smallest kindness graciously rewarded.

The grammatical-historical commentators identify the “little ones” and weigh the gift. Gill marks the descent “from prophets to righteous men, and from righteous men to… those of the lowest… class” — the poorest and least esteemed disciples; Barnes: they are “little” in the world’s estimation and their own, “learners, not yet teachers.” The point, Barnes adds, is the motive: few would refuse cold water to the thirsty, but to give it “because he was a Christian” shows “love to the Saviour himself.” Robertson notes the same idiom — “in the name of a disciple” — for “simple believers… just learners.” Clarke illustrates from the East, where a cup of water given to travellers is no small charity, and cites the rabbinic promise to him who feeds a student of the law; even the poorest may thus “exercise the works of mercy,” for “it is the name of Jesus that… renders services… comparatively contemptible, of high worth.”

The Latin fathers and others widen and warm the promise. Jerome notes the Lord says “cold water” lest poverty plead lack of fuel — removing “even this plea” — so the giver “loses not his reward.” The Gloss: “God looks more to the pious mind of the giver, than to the abundance of the thing given.” Ellicott extends the words, by the light of Matthew 25:40, to “every act of kindness… in the name of that humanity” Christ shares with those “he is not ashamed to call His brethren” (Hebrews 2:11). So the discourse that began with the sending of the Twelve ends with the assurance that not the least cup of water, given for Christ’s sake, shall go unrewarded.

The witnesses make the chapter close on the smallest possible deed graciously crowned. Barnes’s summary draws the lesson of the whole: “what more easy than to give a cup of water to a stranger; and what more easy than to know from what motive we do it!” — yet how many “would lose eternal life rather than do it with a view of honouring Christ.” The “little ones” are deliberately the least — poor, weak, “learners” — that none may plead inability; even “the most impoverished of the poor” can give cold water (Clarke). It is “the name of Jesus that… renders services… comparatively contemptible, of high worth.” Kindnesses to his disciples are “valued in Christ’s books, not according to the cost of the gift, but according to the love… of the giver” (Henry), so that “the widow’s mite… was stamped high” (Luke 21:4). The discourse of the sword ends in a cup of water — and a sure reward.
